file_name: Reddy et al. - 2026 - AI-enhanced skin cancer screening system using CNN on edge devices for real-time and explainable dia.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed an AI-enhanced skin cancer screening system using a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model deployed via TensorFlow Lite on a Raspberry Pi edge device. The AI method was used to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions into benign and suspicious categories, achieving an accuracy of approximately 92-94%. Additionally, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was employed for explainable AI to visualize regions influencing the model's decisions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and deployment of an AI-enhanced skin cancer screening system using a CNN on a Raspberry Pi edge device. It includes a specific methodology (image acquisition, preprocessing, TensorFlow Lite deployment), reports original results (training accuracy 94.2%, validation accuracy 92.8%, test accuracy 91.5%, inference times 0.5–1.2 seconds), and describes a novel technical implementation for real-time, offline diagnosis. Although it cites prior literature, its primary purpose is to report new technical results and system validation rather than summarizing existing work.

title: AI-Enhanced Skin Cancer Screening System Using CNN on Edge Devices for Real-Time and Explainable Diagnosis
journal: International Journal of Emerging Research in Science, Engineering, and Management
brief_summary: This study developed an AI-enhanced skin cancer screening system using a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deployed on a Raspberry Pi edge device for real-time, offline diagnosis. The system captures dermoscopic images via a camera module and classifies skin lesions into benign and suspicious categories using TensorFlow Lite. Evaluation results demonstrated a test accuracy of 91.5%, with inference times ranging from 0.5 to 1.2 seconds per image. To enhance interpretability, Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) was integrated to visualize regions influencing the model's decisions. The system offers a cost-effective, portable solution suitable for rural healthcare settings, costing approximately 20 times less than traditional dermoscopy equipment.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as Class 0 in the binary classification task; characterized by low and uniform activation in Grad-CAM heatmaps.
Classified as Class 1 in the binary classification task; characterized by strong activation in irregular regions in Grad-CAM heatmaps; requires further clinical evaluation.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign (Class 0) and suspicious (Class 1) categories.
all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification using a probability threshold of 0.5. It classifies dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: benign (non-cancerous) and suspicious (requiring further clinical evaluation). The system outputs confidence scores categorized as high (>0.80 or <0.20), medium, or low, but the primary task is discrete class prediction, not continuous regression.

datasets_used.description: A large dermoscopic image dataset mentioned as a source for training the CNN model.

use.training: Used to train the CNN model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset containing more than 10,000 dermoscopic images categorized into seven common types of skin lesions, mentioned as a source for training the CNN model.

ISIC.notes: The model was trained on large dermoscopic image datasets including ISIC and HAM10000. The paper reports overall accuracy metrics for the binary classification task (benign vs. suspicious) rather than per-dataset breakdowns.

HAM10000.notes: The model was trained on large dermoscopic image datasets including ISIC and HAM10000. The paper reports overall accuracy metrics for the binary classification task (benign vs. suspicious) rather than per-dataset breakdowns.

Edge Device Deployment (Raspberry Pi).notes: The system achieved real-time inference with processing times ranging from 0.5 to 1.2 seconds per image, including capture, preprocessing, inference, and visualization. It supports high-throughput screening suitable for rural healthcare camps. The system is approximately 20 times more affordable than traditional dermoscopy equipment.

conclusions: The proposed AI-enhanced skin cancer screening system successfully classifies skin lesions into benign and suspicious categories with an accuracy of approximately 92–94%.

The system achieves real-time inference with processing times ranging from 0.5 to 1.2 seconds per image, enabling high-throughput operation suitable for rural healthcare centers and mobile medical camps.
Deploying the lightweight TensorFlow Lite CNN on a Raspberry Pi edge device eliminates the need for cloud connectivity, ensuring offline operation, reduced latency, and enhanced patient data privacy.
The system is cost-effective, costing approximately ₹18,000–₹20,000, which is about 20 times cheaper than traditional dermoscopy equipment.
Integration of Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) provides visual explanations of model decisions, improving clinical trust and interpretability for healthcare professionals.

The system addresses limitations of existing cloud-based AI solutions, such as high latency, internet dependency, and lack of explainability, by combining edge computing, deep learning, and explainable AI.
The system is portable and user-friendly, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained environments where access to dermatological expertise is limited.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the model was trained and tested on standard datasets (ISIC, HAM10000) without evidence of generalization to independent, real-world clinical populations.

The HAM10000 dataset, used for training, is known to have class imbalance and limited diversity in skin types, which may introduce population bias and affect model generalization across diverse demographics.

The paper claims 'strong sensitivity and specificity' in the conclusion but does not report the actual numerical values for these metrics, making it impossible to assess the trade-off between false positives and false negatives.
The paper does not report key hyperparameters (optimizer, loss function, learning rate, epochs) or the specific loss function used, limiting reproducibility.
The dataset split details (training/validation/testing counts) are not provided, raising potential concerns about data leakage or insufficient testing data size.

The system is a proof-of-concept prototype built with low-cost components (Raspberry Pi, camera module); its reliability and diagnostic accuracy in uncontrolled, real-world clinical environments remain unverified.
The paper received no specific grant funding, which may indicate limited resources for extensive clinical trials or large-scale validation.
The inference time reported in the conclusion (0.9–1.5 seconds) contradicts the results section (0.5–1.2 seconds), indicating inconsistency in reporting performance metrics.

The binary classification task (benign vs. suspicious) simplifies the complex differential diagnosis of skin cancer, potentially missing critical distinctions between different types of malignant lesions (e.g., melanoma vs. BCC/SCC).
